title: Why transitions between activities are so destabilizing
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The moment you dread

You know the feeling. You're sitting on the couch, maybe scrolling your phone, maybe staring at the wall. And you know you need to get up. You need to shower, or make dinner, or go to work, or pick up your kid from school. But your body won't move. Your brain is screaming at you to just stand up, but something in you is frozen. And the longer you sit there, the worse it gets. The guilt builds. The shame creeps in. You start telling yourself you're lazy, broken, defective. I've been there. I've sat in that chair for forty-five minutes, knowing I needed to pee, but unable to bridge the gap between sitting and standing. It's not laziness. It's not a character flaw. It's your nervous system responding to a threat it can't name.

Here's the thing. Transitions between activities are one of the most destabilizing moments for people with complex trauma. And almost no one talks about it. We talk about triggers like loud noises or certain smells or people yelling. But we don't talk about the simple act of switching from one thing to another. And for those of us with a history of trauma, that transition can feel like walking off a cliff.

I want you to think about what happens in your body when you have to change activities. Maybe it's the end of the workday and you have to switch to being a parent. Maybe it's the moment you finish a conversation and have to start a chore. Maybe it's leaving the house to go to the grocery store. Notice what just happened in your body. Did your shoulders tense? Did your stomach tighten? Did your breath get shallow? That's your nervous system sounding an alarm.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say that what you're experiencing is a state of neuroception. Your nervous system is scanning for safety and danger, and a transition feels like an unknown. The brain doesn't know what's coming next. And for a trauma survivor, the unknown is not exciting. It's terrifying. Your system has learned that change often means danger. Because in your past, changes were sudden, unpredictable, and painful. So your body locks down to protect you.

Why your brain treats transitions like threats

Let me be direct with you. Your brain is not trying to sabotage you. It's trying to keep you alive. When you've experienced complex trauma, your nervous system gets wired for survival in a specific way. It learns to predict danger based on very subtle cues. And one of those cues is the gap between activities. The transition itself becomes a trigger.

Think about it this way. If you grew up in a chaotic or abusive environment, you never knew what would happen next. A parent could be calm one moment and explosive the next. A safe moment could turn dangerous without warning. Your brain learned that transitions were dangerous because they often preceded something bad. So now, as an adult, when you have to switch from one activity to another, your brain goes into high alert. It's preparing for the worst. And that preparation looks like freezing, dissociating, or feeling overwhelmed.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets stored in the body. It's not just a memory in your mind. It's a physical response that gets triggered by sensations, movements, and even the anticipation of change. When you're stuck in a transition, your body is actually completing an incomplete survival response from the past. You're frozen because part of you is still in that old moment, waiting for the danger to pass.

Stay with me here. This is important. The transition itself is not the problem. The problem is that your nervous system is still living in the past. It's treating a neutral moment like a life-or-death situation. And the more you fight it, the worse it gets. Because fighting your nervous system only confirms to your brain that there's a threat. You're telling yourself, "I can't handle this transition, something is wrong with me." And your brain hears, "Yes, there is danger, I was right to be afraid."

I remember a time when I was trying to leave my apartment to go to a coffee shop to write. I stood by the door for twenty minutes. My hand was on the doorknob. But I couldn't turn it. My heart was racing. My palms were sweating. I was telling myself, "Just open the door, it's just a door, you've done this a thousand times." But my body was not listening. I finally sat down on the floor and cried. Not because I was sad. Because I was exhausted from fighting myself. And that's when I realized I needed to stop fighting.

The cost of pushing through

Here's where the wellness industry has failed us. They tell us to push through. To just do it. To overcome our resistance with willpower. But that advice is not just unhelpful. It's actively harmful for trauma survivors. Because pushing through a freeze response doesn't release the freeze. It just adds more stress on top of it. You're asking a system that's already in survival mode to work harder. And eventually, it breaks.

I've worked with people who spent years trying to "power through" their transition difficulties. They developed chronic fatigue, migraines, digestive issues, and autoimmune problems. Their bodies were screaming at them to slow down, but they kept pushing. And their nervous systems kept getting more dysregulated. A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk explains exactly how this works. Your body remembers what your mind tries to forget. And when you ignore your body's signals, you pay a price.

Think about what happens when you successfully force yourself through a transition that your nervous system was resisting. You make it to the grocery store. You get the food. You come home. But you're exhausted. More exhausted than the task warrants. Because you spent a huge amount of energy overriding your own survival response. And that energy debt accumulates. Over days, weeks, years. Eventually, you have nothing left.

I'm not saying you should never do hard things. I'm saying you need to understand what's happening in your body when you do them. The goal is not to eliminate transitions. The goal is to make them feel safe enough for your nervous system to allow them. And that requires a completely different approach than just pushing harder.

How to actually work with transition distress

Let me give you something practical. Something that actually works for the nervous system, not just for the thinking brain. The first step is to stop judging yourself for the freeze. I know that's hard. I know you've been told your whole life that you should just be able to switch gears like everyone else. But you're not everyone else. Your nervous system has a different history. And it needs different care.

When you notice yourself stuck in a transition, the first thing to do is pause. Don't try to force yourself to move. Don't argue with yourself. Just notice. What are you feeling in your body? Where is the tension? What is the temperature of your skin? What is your breath doing? This is not about fixing anything. It's about yourself in that moment. Dan Siegel calls this "name it to tame it." When you name what's happening, your prefrontal cortex comes back online. And your survival brain can start to relax.

One technique that many people find helpful is to use a weighted blanket during moments of transition distress. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure stimulation can help your nervous system feel grounded and safe. It's like a hug for your nervous system. I keep one on my couch for those moments when I'm stuck and need to regulate before I can move.

Another approach is to create ritual around transitions. Your nervous system craves predictability. So instead of just switching from one activity to another, create a small bridge. Maybe you take three deep breaths before you leave the house. Maybe you put your hand on your chest and say, "I am safe, I am allowed to move slowly." Maybe you drink a glass of water and feel the sensation of it moving through your body. These small rituals tell your nervous system, "We are transitioning now, and it is safe."

I also want to mention that supplements can sometimes support nervous system regulation, especially if you're dealing with chronic stress. One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium is involved in hundreds of biochemical reactions in the body, including those related to stress and relaxation. But please, talk to your doctor before starting any supplement. And never use supplements as a replacement for addressing the underlying nervous system patterns.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises specifically designed to help you understand and work with your triggers, including transition-related ones. Sometimes having a structured guide can make the process feel less overwhelming.

Here's the most important thing I can tell you. Transitions will probably always be harder for you than for people without trauma. That's not a failure. That's a fact of your biology. And accepting that fact is the first step toward actually living with it. You don't have to fix yourself. You don't have to become someone who transitions effortlessly. You just have to learn how to support yourself through the difficulty. And that is a skill you can learn.

I still struggle with transitions. Some days I can move from one activity to another with relative ease. Other days I sit in my car for fifteen minutes before I can get out. And on those days, I don't fight it. I sit. I breathe. I tell myself, "This is where I am right now." And eventually, my body lets me move. Not because I forced it. But because I stopped making it defend itself against me.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's foundational book on how trauma affects the body and brain.
  • YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link) - A weighted blanket that can help calm the nervous system during moments of distress.
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A practical workbook with exercises for understanding and managing C-PTSD triggers.
  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link) - A magnesium supplement that may support nervous system relaxation.
